data.title: 230 Tankers Stuck In Hormuz - The CPI Is Showing The Real Cost
data.description:
Iran is doing a very poor job, dishonorable some would say, of allowing Oil to go through the Strait of Hormuz. That is not the agreement we have! - President Donald Trump

Written by Sam Kovacs

Introduction: Where I was wrong

I have spent most of the past 6 weeks tracking the Iran conflict and mapping out its relevance to markets and our portfolios. I've presented multiple scenarios, and on Monday I explored the scenario I thiought seemed most likely: no ceasefire.

Many members relished in the opportunity to comment "this aged well" in the aftermath. That's fine. I get to gloat when I'm right, you get to bash me when I'm wrong. It's a good deal.

As you all know we got a tentative ceasefire. I didn't see diplomacy prevailing, but we avoided a worst case scenario which I thought we were heading towards.

Both parties claimed a victory on their own state media, which led me to believe that both parties didn't quite agree on what they signed.

The most important at any point is to update our market views as the situation evolves and in this piece I will give my emerging market views.

72 hours after the ceasefire

Trump has claimed that the ceasefire was contingent on "COMPLETE, IMMEDIATE and SAFE OPENING" of the Strait.

Iran's Araghchi has agreed to a ceasefire with "safe passage via coordination with Iran's Armed Forces".

It was quite clear that while both parties were willing to cease hostilities for a short time, it wasn't on the same conditions.

Iran says the US accepted continuing Iranian control of the Strait. The White house has suggested that this is "completely absurd".

This could have been the first warning sign that we might not have quite the situation that we thought we have.

Anyways, let's look at 72 hours of evidence.

Around a dozen ships have moved through the ship in 2 days, of which four were dry cargo rather than tankers. Before the war this was around 130 per day which means that the traffic has maybe picked up to at most 4-5% of what it used to be. That's not a complete opening.

ADNOC CEO said that 230 loaded tankers are still stuck in Hormuz, that the Strait is not open.

Iran has effectively confirmed sea mines and told ships to take "alternative routes to avoid collisions".

Tolls are exceeding $1mn per ship despite Trump saying Iran "better not" be collecting it.

Effectively, traffic has not picked up since ceasefire. I've been saying this since the beginning of the conflict. A ceasefire cannot equal an opening of the Strait without a significant lag, which is of multiple weeks as a best case scenario.

And in a show of just how well the ceasefire has held, Saudi's East-West pipeline was damaged by an Iranian drone, curtailing throughput by 700,000bpd, just hours after the ceasefire.

Kuwait has also accused Iran of drone attacks despite the ceasefire.

Then there is also the fact that Israel hasn't stopped its military operations on Lebanese civilian infrastructure.

Vice President Vance has conceded that there was a "a legitimate misunderstanding" over the terms of the ceasefire.

The US and Israel are of the opinion that Lebanon isn't included in the ceasefire, Iran that it was. Iran's Ghalibaf is saying that talks are unreasonable as the US has broken 3 of the 10 conditions.

Today the so called Islamabad talks have started. Both parties are arguing about what they have agreed to.

In the meantime, Hormuz remains shut with mines in the Strait.

The Supply Hole

The EIA has indicated that over 9mn bpd will be shut in behind Hormuz in April. This is increased from 7.5mn bpd in March, and by far the largest supply shock in history, double both the 1973 and 1990 supply shocks.

Goldman has already gone on record calling it the "largest supply shock in history".

If everything goes well and according to plan, we might expect a pre-crisis situation to return by the end of the year. That is the BEST case scenario. The market is still living on hopium of that reality.

Which is fine, the market can aggressively discount the future today, Brent can drop 13% in a day, stocks can jump 2.5%, in a hope that the crisis is over.

But with Brent at $95 vs $70 pre war, it should be clear that the situation hasn't quite resolved, and that the implications for inflation and the economy need to be derived from that.

The pre-war consensus of oil prices in the low $60s are dead. EIA now sees oil peaking at $115 in Q2 and reaching below $90 by Q4, Goldman has a risk situation at $93 by Q4, and JP Morgan has a target of $150 if the shut-in continues until May.

My second scenario labelled S2 in the chart below, is the one that suggests that the ceasefire holds but opening Hormuz isn't as easy planned. It's becoming the base scenario. The risk scenario was my former base: ceasefire doesn't hold and we move back to open fire and kinetic warfare.

The base case scenario is quite simple at this point: Brent remains at $90-$100 well throughout the 3 remaining quarters of 2026.

Today's CPI. A cool core is a distraction.

The reason structurally elevated oil prices throughout the rest of the year remains a difficult proposition is that oil is the first entrant of the capitalist system, and of inflation.

Everything is downstream: the price of oil increases the price of transportation of goods and of humans, it increases the prices of plastics which are everywhere. There are knock on effects in the pricing of food (due to fertilizer costs), goods, services, and eventually wages, as workers demand more compensation to pay for the more expensive everything.

We don't want this.

The CPI print came in at +0.9% MoM, and +3.3% YoY. This was in line with the consensus, and the headlines that took over were that the core CPI came in at 2.6% YoY which was below consensus of 2.7%.

The market is treating this as good news about inflation with the index having opened the day in the green.

It's not really good news and it reflects more of the timing of the knock on effects to inflation.

Every round of inflation the media overlooks the easy causal chain of events. You can look back to my 2021 articles warning of the inflation that was coming.

We called that before even Larry Summers and other "experts" did. I was just a young guy Scuba diving in Bali during the pandemic, yet those willing to connect the factual dots (rather than the narrative dots) can see the emerging picture.

In March, the CPI impact has been derived purely from gas prices. The prices at the pump have increased by 30%, but all other categories have yet to absorb the shock.

The bulk of the CPI repricing is happening in April to June prints. The ISM Services Prices Paid index shot up from 63 to 70, which is the largest one month jump since 2012, and the highest level since October 2022. We now see that service businesses are repricing actively right now. The survey is looking forward for April, and means we'll see inflation repricing.

Inflation expectations over the upcoming year have jumped from 3.4% to 3.8% according to the Michigan survey of consumers. This means that inflation expectations are unanchoring, which is dangerous as it creates the feedback loops which push inflation up.

United airlines (UAL) has warned of 20% fare hikes. UPS (UPS), Amazon (AMZN), and Fedex (FDX) have all warned of "temporary" fuel surcharges.

The Farm bureau has warned of fertilizer shortages having occured due to the war. Missing spring spraying means knock on effects.

These cover airfare, logistics, and food, and the knock on effects are happening in April, May, and June.

My base case and my risk case are higher than what is implied by the EIA of oil fading and inflation peaking next month.

All 3 scenarios are a mile away from the pre war predictions. This supply shock has barely been digested at this point.

Today's core CPI is still yesterday's news. Even a total ceasefire and reopening today would still cause an inflation spike which will need to be resolved with, and puts the Fed in a bind.

The cards above show the change in the situation. The market has largely brushed this off and ignored this.

I believe this is a mistake.

The 3 structural changes that aren't priced

The truth is that the Hormuz risk premium has become at best semi-permanent. Iran is weaponizing the strait, it has survived the first 40 days of the conflict all while retaining control over Hormuz.

If that wasn't true: why is Iran charging sky high toll fees?

Remember earlier when I said oil is the first input of the capitalist system? Well the outcome of this war is that Iran has laid a claim to a toll on 20% of the world's supply of that very input.

First, The Hormuz risk premium doesn't return to zero. Countries are going to be buying oil from elsewhere as it becomes available to them (Canada, Brazil, US, and Texas). Past supply shocks in the area are very good proof of this. The risk was recalculated, we don't just forget what happened in the past 40 days.

Second, the reacceleration of inflation goes in the face of the entire consensus view of a soft landing that justified a 7,000 SPX (or thereabouts) in the first place.

Once the second order effects of inflation transmission start to kick-in, we will realize that we're between a rock and a hard place.

Third, the central bank is trapped without a way out. The economy is flat-lining and likely to weaken so the Fed won't hike rates. But with inflation reaccelerating it won't cut either.

We risk moving into stagflationary wait-and-see at the Fed. That's a mile away from the "inflation is benign and rates should be cut" environment we conceived we were in at the onset of the year.

So what wins?

We must ask ourselves what wins in the scenario that the data is starting to paint quite convincingly regardless of whether the current ceasefire holds or not.

If it doesn't hold, it's a more aggressive version of what is already in the tea leaves.

What does well when oil is $95-$100, headline inflation is rising, and rates are frozen?

Well to start with the Brazilian Real does well. We identified Brazil as a strong theme in 2026 without the commodity wildcard, but the elevated commodity prices really support the Brazilian economy.

The dollar has fell out of the recent technical formation, and a move below $5 could cause significant downwards movement.

I think Petrobras (PBR) is an absolute steal. I've mentioned this quite a few times.

At $21, you're still getting a 7% yield on a company that is starting to get its act together. Yes it's mismanaged, but there is nothing quite like it in the opportunity set today. The stock didn't even blink on the announcement of the ceasefire. Why would that be?

Then you have everything that would be a natural beneficiary in a stagflationary regime: REITs with inflation escalators, Fertilizer producers, and later gold miners once gold finds a bottom and the regime adapts.

Of course energy continues to print cash and we're likely to see special dividends and buybacks throughout the year to redistribute the excess cash.

What doesn't do well is long duration growth, like anything in the magnificent 7 (MAGS), consumer discretionary, or rate dependant small caps.

The reaction on April 8th to the ceasefire showed a market that traded as if the conflict was effectively over. Airlines were up double digits, and energy stocks faded.

The vast majority of scenarios now show Hormuz either shut or under Iranian control, and those scenarios lead to the situation mapped out in this article.

Before the war the trade was to be long duration. That has broken down, and bar a swift resolution with Iran giving up all of its leverage, that trade doesn't make sense. In that case, the reaction is a dead-cat bounce and we'd be well served by making necessary adjustments to our portfolio while we have the chance.

Conclusions

I won't always be right about markets, but it's also why we don't do anything drastic. During the first 40 days of the conflict, we identified the 10 names in our coverage at the Dividend Freedom Tribe (we cover 100 hand picked stocks). These 10 were the weakest on a weighted scenario outcomes as they were dependent on a soft landing scenario, which now has drastically reduced in likelihood.

As we will be doing our monthly transactions over the weekend (to be executed on Monday), we will be taking those off our books and putting stuff we see as more appropriate in.

We're not reshuffling the entire portfolio, just making sure that our convexity lies in scenarios which are the most probable.

data.title: MacroGenics: Oncology Opportunities In Play, But Investing Feels Like A Gamble
data.description:
Investment Overview

MacroGenics (MGNX) is a Rockville, Maryland-based biotech, and an investment opportunity I am returning to, admittedly with some trepidation. Five years ago, I covered the company in a note for Seeking Alpha, assigning stock a Buy rating, and shares have fallen in value by >90% since.

I wrote in March 2021:

MacroGenics' (MGNX) description of its drug development pipeline as "deep and differentiated" is hard to disagree with. It's rare to come across a mid-sized biotech with eight different small molecules in development - 10 if you count two undisclosed targets in preclinical development per a partnership struck with Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) subsidiary Janssen in December last year - or one that leverages so many different technologies and approaches to drug development.

At that time, MGNX stock was trading ~$33 per share. By June 2022 it had sunk in value to $2.3 per share, however, as several of its assets disappointed in the clinic.

For example, a Phase 2/3 study for margetuximab/ retifanlimab combo in HER2+ gastroesophageal junction cancer underwhelmed, and a study of enoblituxumab, targeting the protein protein B7-H3 was halted after a higher than expected number of patient deaths. Partnerships established with Janssen, Johnson & Johnson's (JNJ) drug development subsidiary, and Zai Labs (ZLAB) also failed to ignite, with the former now terminated, and the latter seemingly in abeyance.

Nevertheless, Macrogenics has played a role in developing three FDA approved therapeutics - according to its 2025 annual report / 10-K filing:

ZYNYZ (retifanlimab-dlwr), an anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody (mAb) that we out-licensed; MARGENZA® (margetuximab-cmkb), an anti-HER2 mAb that we sold to a partner; and TZIELD® (teplizumab-mzwv), an anti-CD3 mAb that we sold to a partner.

Furthermore, the company continues to develop products, as we can see above, and its stock hit a 15-month high in March after the company reported its 2025 results and provided business updates on March 9th.

MacroGenics In 2026 - Lorigerlimab Particl Clinical Hold Lifted

Eric Risser, President and CEO of MacroGenics - appointed in August last year, and replacing Scott Koenig, M.D., Ph.D. who stepped down after serving as President and Chief Executive Officer for the past 24 years - was quoted in the biotech's earnings release as follows:

Looking ahead, we anticipate several important milestones in 2026, including initial clinical data from the Phase 1 studies of MGC026 and MGC028, and from the LINNET study of lorigerlimab. Additionally, we plan to submit an IND for MGC030, a first-in-class topoisomerase I inhibitor-based ADC. Finally, with cash runway into late 2027, we believe we are well positioned to execute on our plan and drive meaningful value for our shareholders.

MGC026 and MGC028 are antibody drug conjugates - the former targeting B7-H3 once again, the latter targeting ADAM9, a member of the ADAM family of multifunctional type 1 transmembrane proteins that play a role in tumorigenesis and cancer progression and is overexpressed in multiple solid tumors.

MGC030 targets an undisclosed antigen, and at first glance, I don't feel it would be wise to attach too much value to these candidates given only MGC026 has entered the clinic, but Macrogenics believes its Synaffix - a Lonza (Swiss multinational manufacturing) company technology may afford its candidates a competitive advantage, as shown below (source: corporate presentation):

Further ahead in the development process is lorigerlimab, which blockades two validated checkpoint inhibitor molecules in PD-1 and CTLA-4, which has achieved objective responses observed across multiple indications, including PROC (ovarian) and mCRPC (prostate) cancers, although a Phase 2 study had been placed under a partial clinical hold by the FDA following a patient death.

However, MacroGenics announced on Wednesday (April 8th) that the FDA had lifted its hold - CEO Risser stated in a press release:

With the partial clinical hold lifted, we intend to resume enrollment of new study participants in the ongoing LINNET study and we remain on track to provide a mid-year clinical update on the program.

In response, Macrogenics stock has climbed to a new high of $3.65 at the time of writing - market cap valuation now stands at $231m.

The likes of PD-1 inhibiting Keytruda (pembrolizumab) and Opdivo (nivolumab), marketed and sold by Merck (MRK) and Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), and CTLA-4 targeting Yervoy (ipilimumab), also marketed and sold by BMY, earned respectively ~$31bn, ~$10bn, and ~$2.8bn in 2025, yet, apart from AstraZeneca (AZN), with volrustomig, and Chinese Pharma Akeso, with cadonilimab, no other Pharma or biotech companies are pursuing development of a twin PD-1 CTLA4 inhibitor.

An overview of MacroGenics Phase 2 study plan is shown below:

MacroGenics also shares an overview of data suggesting a combo of PD-1 + CTLA4 may be more effective than PD-1 alone as shown below:

It's important to stress that this is not lorigerlimab data, and we may also note, and that MacroGenics recently ceased development of lorigerlimab in the indication of second-line metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer ("mCRPC"), after management concluded that:

experimental treatment arm would not reach the study’s primary goal of showing an improvement in progression-free survival (rPFS) vs. that of the control arm for the targeted patient population.

Nevertheless, the FDA lifting of its clinical hold does permit MacroGenics to continue its Phase 2 in PROC and CCGyn patients, and the upcoming study update ought to be a catalysts worth tracking.

What's The Play On MacroGenics Today?

I have to admit that Macrogenics' safety track record makes me wonder if the company is too cavalier in its approach, however its candidates treat very sick patients and it seems most ADC developers are struggling with the toxicity of their products, including the likes of Merck and Daiichi, whose partnership is worth up to >$20bn.

Macrogenics has a partnership in place with Gilead Sciences (GILD) (whose own oncology track record is unimpressive), worth up to $1.6bn in milestone payouts, focused on developing T-cell engagers ("TCEs"), another promising field of development in oncology - Amgen's Imdelltra (tarlatamab) is a TCE that secured approval in small cell lung cancer ("SCLC"), earning >$500m of revenues last year.

MagroGenics could potentially earn close to $1bn in payouts potentially due from Sanofi (SNY), Incyte (INCY), and Tersera in relation to Tzield, Zynyz, and Margenza, although I would be circumspect on the chances of such milestones being achieved - Tzield earned just $63m of revenues in 2025, Zynyz ~$66m, and Margenza likely substantially less (the drug earned $16.4m revenues in 2024, before MacroGenics sold it to biotech Tersera Therapeutics last year).

Macrogenics' catalysts due in 2026 are shown below:

In my view, Macrogenics must deliver a study success this year or next to futureproof the company, as it will need to raise further funding to stretch its funding runway beyond 2027. The company did earn $149.5m of revenues in 2025, $52.6m of which was related to contract manufacturing, making a net loss of $(74.6m). Cash position was reported as $190m.

To date, Macrogenics hasn't struck gold, as its valuation back in 2021 suggested it might, but the company has not been a total bust either, and its disease targets are fields of significant unmet need where we may make the point, no other biotech or pharma has had major success improving survival rates.

Perhaps the fact I have been following Macrogenics for such a long time makes me slightly biased, but with a <$250m market cap at the time of writing, it's clear the market assigns little value to the current pipeline, meaning that if a study is successful now or in 2027, the upside potential runs into the triple-digit percentages (in my view), and I don't see the company being forced to delist or declare bankruptcy in the foreseeable future, so near term downside seems limited at this time.

As MacroGenics is a <$500m market cap biotech, we must stress that is significant risk attached to any investment in the company. Based on current burn rate ($14.2M in Q4, $74.6M in 2025), and management commentary, we must note the funding runway does not extend beyond 2027, and without a study success of note, MacroGenics could find itself unable to raise funding via share offerings, or be able to raise debt at a competitive rate.

Nevertheless, there are meaningful catalysts to look forward, as discussed in this post, perhaps beginning with a full lorigerlimab when the company announces Q1 earnings around May 13th. Hope springs eternal, but backing MacroGenics today does feel like a gamble, and therefore I'd assign a Hold rating for the time being.

data.title: Restaurant Brands International: Valuation Is Nearly Cooked
data.description:
After covering Restaurant Brands International, Inc. or simply RBI (QSR) for more than a year, it’s so nice to see it finally making a bullish breakout. This will be my fifth analysis on RBI, and since the first time I covered it, the price has already risen by 27%. And from my previous coverage almost four months ago, it has already given 12% returns and justified my bullish ratings. This is why I believe that it’s essential to reassess the stock one month before it releases its Q1 2026 earnings. It is more important today if we consider increasing macroeconomic volatility amid stubborn inflation and intense oil price swings. Additionally, valuation is beginning to show limited upside potential. Meanwhile, technicals stay bullish but seem to signal early caution.

RBI Has Stayed Robust Lately

Fast-food restaurant stocks have faced various challenges in the past year due to stubborn inflation and stiff competition that continues to squeeze their pricing flexibility and margins. Even established brands like Burger King, Tim Horton’s, and Popeye’s are no exception as they compete with other large and popular players like McDonald’s (MCD) and Starbucks (SBUX). Despite this, their owner, Restaurant Brands International, Inc. exudes resilience with its effective strategies and efforts to maintain customer loyalty and market positioning of its brands. This was visible in its most recent performance.

In Q4 2025, its operating revenues amounted to $2.47B, up by 7.4% YoY from $2.30B. This YoY growth was also higher than in my previous coverage at 7.0%, showing stability despite a challenging market environment. Of course, we cannot deny the fact that YoY growth has shrunk in the past two years. Note that its YoY growth was a double-digit number. It climbed continuously to 24% in Q3 2024, 26% in Q4 2024, and 21% in Q1 2025. But since Q2 2025, we have seen how it has weakened again from 15% down to its current growth rate. This should not be surprising because many fast-food restaurants have suffered from softer consumer demand amid stubborn inflation. They also had to indirectly compete with cheaper fast-casual restaurants like Shake Shack (SHAK) as their price gap narrowed. Nonetheless, the notion that it improved a bit from my previous coverage shows QSR’s resilience. Additionally, it may be part of its capacity management to optimize sales versus costs and restaurant count.

For instance, BK’s restaurant count decreased to 7,025 from 7,082 YoY. However, its systemwide sales rose to $2.97B or 1.9% YoY, which may show its strategic restaurant management. It had to close underperforming restaurants and focused on high-traffic locations. With that, the average sales of Burger King per restaurant reached $422,776 ($2.97B/7,025 restaurants) versus $411,607 YoY. Meanwhile, TH maintained a strong demand as sales continued to rise faster amid its expansion. This may tell us that its increasing global market penetration has been fruitful. In fact, the average sales per restaurant reached $418,229 ($1.92B/4,586 restaurants) versus $410,443. The same applies to Firehouse Subs. The strengths in these three brands completely offset the weakness in Popeye’s. As a result, the overall average sales per restaurant were $367,150 ($12.1B/33,041 restaurants) versus $351,098 YoY.

RBI Total Systemwide Sales Restaurant Count Average Sales Per Restaurant
Q4 2024 $11.3B 32,125 $351,098
Q4 2025 $12.1B 33,041 $367,150

The challenge lies in its mounting cost pressures as stubborn inflation continued to raise prices. It was more evident in its company-owned restaurants since RBI shouldered the day-to-day expenses of these restaurants. On a lighter note, its franchising segment remained steady and even more profitable as franchise and advertising expenses decreased. This helped partially offset the impact of higher cost of sales and company-owned restaurant expenses. So, its operating margin remained high and a double-digit figure at 25.7% despite decreasing from 27.6% YoY.

Risk Factors

Fast-food restaurants like RBI face various risks in the current market environment. One factor to consider is stubborn inflation. Although it has already slowed down, it remains above the 2.0% Fed target. This also means that prices are still increasing. Tariff woes may exacerbate it due to their tendency to raise domestic prices. With that, weaker purchasing power and higher cost pressures may affect its growth and margins this year. On top of that, skyrocketing oil prices may have negative spillovers. Why? It will raise the price of fuel, which will immediately affect large and asset-heavy industries like shipping, freight and logistics, industrial, and manufacturing. The added costs incurred by many costs may be passed on to consumers and intensify inflation.

Moreover, competition is still intense as pricing wars remain prevalent. This should not be surprising since fast-food restaurants typically capitalize on cheaper pricing rather than value propositions. It has to watch out for MCD, which will launch its new cheaper value meals to combat rising prices by attracting more volume. Notable examples are its Under $3 Menu and the $4 Breakfast Meal Deal. I believe this will be challenging for many peers to emulate because of MCD’s vast market presence and brand popularity. Also, Burger King has fewer restaurants on a YoY basis, which is opposite to the increasing MCD restaurants.

What Makes QSR Well-Positioned

One thing I like about QSR or RBI strong is its strategic restaurant management. You can see how it was able to sustain its sales growth in Burger King despite the lower restaurant count YoY. This can tell us its prudent management and concentration on high-foot-traffic and/or in-demand restaurants to avoid unnecessary costs. I believe it also helped it maintain an operating margin above 20% despite the stubborn inflation. If you look at its adjusted operating margin of 31.6% versus 20.7% YoY, you can say that it was also able to reduce its costs in Burger King.

Burger King Systemwide Sales Restaurant Count Average Sales Per Restaurant
Q4 2024 $2.9B 7,082 $411,607
Q4 2025 $3.0B 7,025 $422,776

Another factor is its well-balanced business model. It knows how to balance owned and franchised restaurants, which is crucial amid the rising prices. Running owned restaurants means you get total control over strategies and earnings, but you also carry all expenses and risks. Meanwhile, franchising only give you a portion of the sales of franchised restaurants. But you won’t have to shoulder their day-to-day expenses. Also, fees are from their sales, not operating or net income. So, you have a secure revenue stream.

Lastly, RBI keeps robust liquidity. Cash and cash equivalents are still ample at $1.16B, which can cover the combined amount of accounts payable and borrowings maturing within a year. In addition, the debt maturity profile is still decent because 99% of the total amount will not mature within the year. In fact, most of the amount has a maturity of at least three years. My only concern is that its Net Debt/EBITDA is quite high at 4.4x, which means that the company needs 4.4 years theoretically to pay the whole amount even if it doesn’t have to. This also means that RBI has moderately high leverage. Anyway, RBI can still manage it through strategic issuance to support its repayments. Hence, I believe it still has enough buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update the Dividend Discount Model. The annualized dividend is now $2.60 based on the most recent quarterly payout of $0.65. So, the dividend growth rate is also higher at 4.20% using the five-year CAGR. Meanwhile, I raised its risk premium to 5.5% to be more conservative and price in more risks. Lastly, its Beta decreased to 0.56, which means that it has become less volatile in the past three months. This also shows that the price swings of RBI have been more manageable than the S&P 500. With all these numbers, my new TP or target price is $79.27. This justifies the recent price rally. However, its gap to the current price is already narrow, which suggests limited upside potential. So, I suggest extra caution.

Technicals

RBI’s technicals have remained bullish as the price increases with higher highs and lows. It also trends above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which means that the current momentum is stronger than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. In addition, the 50-day SMA is trending upwards, which suggests that recent market sentiments have stayed optimistic.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI warrant some caution as their uptrend slows down. This can tell us that fewer buyers are entering as the stock becomes nearly fully priced. The MACD line is still increasing and remains within positive territory, which means that buying volume still outpaces selling volume. But you can see that its recent highs have flattened. More importantly, its RSI signal early bearish divergence. The price high increase from December 8 to April 8, but the indicator decreased. This means that there were fewer bulls who have entered recently. This should not be surprising because the stock is approaching the overbought region. If you look at the selling volume yesterday, you can infer that some investors are already taking profits. Hence, I advise you to stay on the sidelines.

Conclusion

Restaurant Brands International, Inc. remains robust despite the challenges surrounding the market. Its decent growth, stable margins, and liquidity show its prudent restaurant management and improved efficiency to ensure sustainability. However, valuation now shows limited upside as it comes close to my TP. Technicals also advise some caution due to early bearish divergence signals and potential overbuying. I’m downgrading my rating on this stock from buy to hold.

data.title: SanDisk's Rally Is Backed By Something Bigger
data.description:
Introduction

SanDisk (SNDK) has become a fascinating memory story, although a complex one in terms of how much one should chase a stock after such a dramatic movement as the dynamics have clearly shifted. This is not a simple story about guessing whether the company survives in a poor NAND backdrop anymore following the spin-off. SanDisk has overcome that hurdle already. The only relevant question at this point is whether we are paying for a cyclical spike in economics or for structural improvement and this is the only discussion that matters.

NAND Demand Is Starting to Look Structural

The fundamental difference between the environment and the memory bull market lies in the fact that the management no longer talks about the commodity nature of the NAND industry. According to SanDisk's Q2 earning release, there is an increased level of capacity management and planning on the part of the data centers because of their significance in driving demand.

I was impressed by Q2 earnings with revenue at $3.03 billion, representing a rise of 31% from the prior quarter. In addition, gross profit as per the non-GAAP standard came to around 51%. The Q3 revenue forecast by the management is between $4.4 billion and $4.8 billion along with gross margins between 65% and 67%.

In addition to the interesting dynamics above, SanDisk has provided an industrial reason to believe in continued pricing support in the medium term. Namely, the company pointed out that data center revenues increased 64% sequentially in Q2 and the company's enterprise SSD demand is growing faster than ever before amid accelerating adoption of hyperscalers, OEMs, and system builders.

Meanwhile, management highlighted that Q3 will be relatively undersupplied in comparison with Q2. In summary, the company does not try to drive volumes just for the sake of volume; its growth targets remain mid to high teens bit growth and any significant capex ramp-up needs attractive economic visibility.

NAND’s AI Shift: Demand Not Just Pricing Is Driving the Story

As I mentioned earlier, the stock has become a pure play on NAND and as always in this business, pricing is an essential metric. However, I would not frame the SanDisk thesis in purely pricing terms. I believe that the company benefits from an increasing relevance of NAND demand in AI which means that the most attractive markets will continue growing and supporting profitability for players like SanDisk. To be specific, management mentioned that it expects data centers to become the largest NAND market in 2026 which is a significant change of dynamics compared to previous years.

Moreover, the company's roadmap provides another reason for a good stock valuation: it is introducing BiCS8 and its PCIe Gen5 TLC drives which were qualified by a second hyperscaler, and will receive additional qualifications going forward. As for the BiCS8 QLC product – Stargate, it is currently in qualification with two major hyperscalers and expected to ramp up production during the next few quarters.

Moreover, the company announced that SanDisk and Kioxia agreed to extend the Yokkaichi joint venture until December 31, 2034, with SanDisk agreeing to pay $1.165 billion from 2026 to 2029 for manufacturing services and continued supply availability. For me, this is an important detail in the current situation since supply security remains a significant factor of the competitive advantage.

Risk Assessment

Nevertheless, I should stress that it is not always easy to find stocks that are worth buying aggressively as SNDK has seen a strong bull run lately. Therefore, the company must now demonstrate impeccable delivery of its strategy, margins expansion, further traction among hyperscalers and no signs of softness in NAND pricing. In other words, we have moved to a situation in which SanDisk needs not only to be right in general terms, but also to be correct with regard to timing.

In other words, although the underlying business has become very attractive, it requires flawless quarterly results. The fact is that once a company starts to move its stock dramatically to the upside, it becomes difficult for investors to give the company any slack, they expect perfection from it. If Q3 turns out to be positive for earnings but Q4 is not discussed quite as positively as before, the stock could easily lose momentum.

Growth Is Underpriced, Even If the Stock Isn’t

SNDK’s valuation does appear to support a bull thesis in a way that is not apparent initially once one adjusts for forward growth expectations, rather than just trailing metrics. On its face, EV/Sales (approximately 7.3x forward) and Price/Sales (approximately 7.3x forward) do seem expensive against peers, which explains poor scores on trailing valuations.

Nonetheless, I prefer to focus on forward EPS and forward growth-adjusted valuations. The forward P/E, for example, is around ~19-20x, lower than industry averages, while the PEG ratio (approximately 0.09) indicates that the market have enough of an expectation for earnings acceleration. This, again, is important because the estimates imply nearly a doubling in revenue (from approximately $15.8 billion in 2026 to approximately $28 billion in 2027), likely due to rapid inflection from the AI-driven NAND demand.

What is also encouraging in my opinion is the form of the estimates, the company is expected to grow rapidly now and then stabilize at a higher base level, which means that the market is not assuming that we have another supercycle ahead. In this context I don't see SanDisk as being cheap per se but rather reasonably priced.

Conclusion

I have no doubt about SanDisk at all but there’s a bit less certainty here, at this price point. Indeed, we see clear positive effects on the structure of the NAND market, thanks to AI applications and data centers. This makes things quite optimistic for the long term. I remain constructive for three reasons: supply discipline, better product strategy, and more hyperscaler deals. On the other hand, I know that people may be more bullish than usual. There’s not much room for mistakes here, but I still think that there are gains to be made.

data.title: Devon Energy: The Easy Money Has Been Made
data.description:
Devon Energy (DVN) has seen some strong rallies in recent weeks starting from around January. The rally has followed oil prices as they climbed toward $95-100 per barrel due to the Hormuz crisis. The move has been sharp but mostly macro-driven, so investors have a valid concern – if the war subsides and oil prices normalize, will DVN go down to its pre-spike range?

In this article I will argue that with the pending Coterra (CTRA) merger announced in early February, DVN stock has found a floor – near current prices – below which it should not fall. The deal gives DVN ~$1B in annual cost savings and capital efficiencies. They also plan to increase dividend by ~31% and expect to authorize a large post-merger new $5B buyback program. These things change Devon’s per-share earnings base and give it a higher valuation floor than previously.

On the other hand, as long as oil holds or rises, Devon has a fair chance of strong – if temporary – upside.

Putting these ideas together, DVN stock now appears fairly valued with an upside bias. The rally we have seen may not hold, but the underlying business is now stronger. I, therefore, consider DVN a Hold at current levels, with possibility of upside if oil prices stay near $100, while downside is supported by structural improvements we can expect from the merger, including the capital returns.

The War Premium

The Hormuz crisis has pushed oil prices higher in recent weeks, with WTI approaching the ~$95–100 range. This is well above the typical mid-cycle prices of ~$65–75 per barrel. The market was otherwise balanced enough, and there was nothing structurally different to sustain a $90-100 pricing without the disruption happening. As proof of that statement, US production remained strong, OPEC was running with spare capacity, and demand growth was normal, not accelerating. So the additional ~$20–30 in oil prices was Iran related. And therefore, probably temporary.

Historically, such premiums tend to be volatile and short-lived – although the exact span depends on the conflict in question. Prices will spike just on the news, and go the other direction, again on the news. Such events may well be opportunities for speculation, but not for sober investment.

That means that, for Devon, part of the recent strength in share prices may be tied to oil prices that may not persist. However, with the Coterra merger, Devon may look different even when oil prices normalize.

Coterra Merger

Devon Energy is strongly oil-weighted, working primarily in the Delaware Basin. Coterra Energy brings in a mix of oil and natural gas assets, and has significant positions in the Permian and Marcellus.

Devon now produces around ~830–850 thousand barrels of oil equivalent per day (Mboe/d). Numerically, Coterra produces around the same, but it has a higher proportion (of gas-weighted production). Coterra’s gas comes primarily from the Marcellus.

When combined, the company will be among the larger U.S. E&P operators, with pro forma production exceeding 1.6 million barrels of oil equivalent per day. The merger also creates a portfolio where capital can be shifted between oil and gas according to demand.

Post-merger, Devon will become a much stronger entity. The merger introduces approximately $1B in annual pre-tax synergies. These savings come because they have substantial and overlapping positions across key shale basins, where they can now allocate capital more efficiently. Since they would be sharing infrastructure across wells, operating costs will reduce. Lastly, corporate overhead will reduce because some functions are essentially duplicative and can be removed.After tax, this translates to roughly ~$750–800 million in incremental annual free cash flow. Given current share outstanding of ~621.44M, that translates to approximately $1.20–1.30 per share in incremental free cash flow. None of this comes from higher oil prices or production growth: this is simply a structural improvement from the merger.

Capital Returns

The merger will vastly change Devon’s capital return profile. It currently pays an annual dividend of ~$0.96 per share, translating to a yield of roughly ~2% at current prices. Management has said they will increase this by ~31% after the merger. That would make the annual dividend around ~$1.26 per share, or a forward yield of ~2.6%.

The increase in dividend is supported by FCF. At ~$70 per barrel, Devon generates around ~$3.1B of annual FCF, around ~$5 per share. If you ignore synergies and buybacks, the post-merger payout ratio comes to 1.26 / 5.0 = ~25.2%. This is broadly similar to the company’s current payout ratio of ~24.5%. That makes the dividend covered, with cash left for reinvestment, debt and buybacks etc.

Buybacks are the second part of the return strategy. Management has said they expect to authorize a new buyback program that would be over $5B in share repurchases. At current ~$48 price, that would retire approximately 104M shares. That would be 16-17% of the current outstanding. Holding FCF constant at $3.1B, before buyback per share FCF was ~$5 while after buyback it increases to ~$6 — a ~20% increase without any change in earnings. If we include merger synergies of ~$800M after tax, total FCF now becomes $3.9B and per share FCF becomes $7.50, or nearly 50% increase.

Valuation

We have seen before that if we consider synergies and buybacks we get a post-merger free cash flow of ~$7.5 per share. Now, at the current share price of ~$48, implied forward FCF multiple is around 6.4x. On a current basis, however, DVN trades at ~12x EV/FCF. Therefore, the FWD multiple is compressed if you go with the $7.5 figure. (For simplicity, I am using per-share FCF multiples as a proxy for equity valuation).

Now, if the market assigns 8x FCF, we have ~$60/share, while if it assigns 10x FCF, we get ~$75/share. This range does not require higher oil prices, production growth – nothing. It only takes in management guidance on synergies and buybacks.

The market is currently discounting the durability of the ~$7.50 per share number. Now, this number derives from synergies and buybacks. The former are operational and within management’s control.

The buybacks come from capital availability. Now, assuming post-merger FCF is $3.9B, and dividend payment comes to around $800M, the company is left with ~$3B in FCF to fund the $5B buyback. Therefore, within a two-year window, this is entirely possible.

It appears that at ~6.4 Fwd FCF multiples, the market is pricing in unreasonable doubt.

A Higher Floor

The idea that Devon now has a higher floor is visible in the numbers. As we have seen, there is now a ~50% increase in per-share earnings power without any corresponding change in oil prices. The key implication is not the upside alone, but the fact that Devon now has less downside-sensitivity.

We can see that using a conservative 6-7 FCF framework and the pre- and post-merger per share FCF values:

We can see that DVN now sits right within the latter range – a range which has been derived without any relation to oil prices. That implies that even if oil prices normalize, DVN share gets support at the $45 level - limiting its downside. Upside remains tied to oil prices, but the downside is now largely decoupled from it.

How about upside, then? At current elevated oil prices, we have seen EV/FCF at around 12x. Even if oil normalizes a little, but still stays stronger than pre-crisis baseline, we can assume 8x or even 10x FCF. Now,

8x FCF → ~$60/share

10x FCF → ~$75/share

That is ~25–55% upside, driven by re-rating, not oil.

What Investors Actually Get

The benefit of this is best seen in where cash flow gets allocated.

At ~$7.50 per share in free cash flow, and a post-merger dividend of approximately ~$1.26 per share, the payout ratio is:

1.26 / 7.50 ≈ ~17%

This is much lower than the current ~24–25% range, because it counts in synergies and buybacks and therefore reflects a more conservative and flexible capital return structure.

That retained ~$6.2+ per share is what funds the buyback. The impact is now straightforward:

  • ~16–17% reduction in share count

  • ~15–20% uplift in per-share metrics

  • higher ownership per investor

The shift from external dependence to internal control is what defines Devon’s higher floor.

Conclusion

Devon’s share price going up recently may have been caused by the war premium, however, my argument is that the Coterra merger gives it a floor beyond which — once having gone up — it will not fall. The merger will fundamentally change the business, producing synergies that didn’t exist earlier. That, plus the buyback, will give it the floor it needs to stay where it is, at the least.

If the market assigns a slightly higher multiple, the stock can have ~25–55% upside from current levels.

Thus, the downside is supported, so this is not a Sell, however, the upside remains tied to oil and potential re-rating, so this is not a hard Buy either. Given that, DVN stock is fairly valued today, and I would rate it a Hold with an upside bias.

data.title: CNH Industrial: Weak Fundamentals And Elevated Valuation Support Continued Downside
data.description:
Following our recent deep dive into Exor N.V. (EXXRF), we are back to analyzing one of its main holdings, CNH Industrial (NYSE:CNH). For our new readers, Exor is the holding company that owns 26.9% of CNH Industrial's economic rights, and we believe it trades at an NAV discount of more than 60%. Going back to CNH Industrial on a standalone basis, in our analysis, we downgraded the company with a sell in June 2025, and this proved to be the right call (Fig. 1). As a reminder, our sell rating was supported by 1) lower guidance, 2) margin pressure combined with limited visibility on earnings and 3) higher inventory levels. With a demanding valuation, our assessment was relatively straightforward.

Fig 1

Why are we still negative?

On a YTD basis, the company's share price increased by almost 25%. Q4 top-line sales increased by 6%, but on a full-year basis, CNH turnover declined by 9%. At the net income level, Q4 profit reached $89 million, with full-year profit of $505 million. This represents an EPS of $0.41. At the current price, the company trades at a P/E of almost 30x. Aside from the valuation, Q4 EPS of $0.19 beat the consensus of $0.10; however, the EPS beat was mainly driven by a pull-forward of Agriculture machinery sales from 2026 to Q4 2025. This was due to Eastern European dealers' higher inventory. Therefore, we are not surprised to see a lower 2026 outlook for the segment. Looking at the press release, the company directly reported that "amid persistent agricultural equipment market challenges, the Company is preparing for lower demand levels in 2026 ahead of the start of an expected industry recovery in 2027." While some may argue that tensions in the Middle East could support agricultural profitability by tightening pesticide supply, we do not expect this to translate into a positive year for the sector. Here at the Lab, we notice a short-term price rebound in soybeans, corn, etc., but we believe there is no change in fundamentals. Therefore, we believe the CNH Agriculture business is still in a wait-and-see phase.

Fig 2

Here at the Lab, we also report an additional downside risk not previously mentioned. Through UBS, we participated in an expert call with a former senior executive at a large EPC contractor. He highlighted the growing severity of construction labor shortages driven by AI CAPEX. According to his view, current constraints are more acute than in previous cycles, driven by strong demand from large-scale projects, particularly data centers, and by reduced labor availability. This tightening in the labor market is likely to pressure margins across CNH Industrial’s customer base and may help explain the weaker margin trajectory observed in the segment. For 2026, the company expects adjusted core operating profit to be between 1% and 2% (Fig. 3). One could argue that the CNH Agriculture business is cyclical. Still, we believe the Construction division could suffer more from AI-related CAPEX competition.

Fig 3

Earnings Change and Valuation

Despite the Q4 beat in the Agriculture segment, the company's guidance reflects ongoing sluggish trends. Even if we might anticipate better results in H2 on geographic mix, lower SG&A, and better inventory management, this is a great story to underwrite. Overall, we have limited visibility into sales for the year, and we do not expect a recovery in 2027. Our initial assessment was to revisit the company with a fresh perspective as our interest in the agricultural segment strengthens; however, we received negative evidence. Additionally, the company will increase its CAPEX, which will reduce its FCF. On an FCF yield, CNH trades at 1.7%. FCF evolution was already a big disappointment in 2024 (it was negative for $400 million). While Q4 was better than feared, part of this result was due to sales being moved from 2026 to Q4 2025. Looking back, we assumed a net profit of $726 million for 2025, but the company delivered a net income of $505 million. With lower sales and a decremental EBIT margin (Fig. 4), we see more downside risks than upside. Even assuming the company’s EPS of $0.4, CNH trades at a 29x P/E in 2026, and our expectations for 2027 are not rosy. This is related to continuous pressure in the CNH construction division and no change in demand in the agriculture segment.

Additionally, we believe the company may trim its dividend. With the current payout, CNH usually pays more than $300 million to shareholders; however, given the CAPEX investment, CNH may need to increase its leverage to finance shareholder remuneration. This is also detrimental to investors. Again, our team usually does not change our valuation methodology. For CNH, this was based on its 5-year historical average (Fig. 5). Last time, we made an exception, applying a 15x P/E recovery multiple (and, continuing to do that, our price target is set at $6 per share, vs. a current market price of $11.7). At this stage, even applying the sector median peers with a 20x P/E, we think CNH is still a sell.

Fig 4

Fig 5

Risks

We have long covered CNH downside risks, and we report additional headwinds in our analysis. The company is exposed to interest rate risk (both on its balance sheet and in its client base). CNH interest rates might also impact its residual value risk in the financial services segment. The company is exposed to changes in commodity prices and lower global consumption. This is related to market cycles in the Agricultural Equipment and Construction segments. CNH risks also include FX and failure in product innovation. As reported in the analysis, CNH might cut its dividend per share due to FCF evolution. This might result in multiple compression.

Conclusion

Higher inflation expectations and a market softening will drag the company’s valuation down in our view. We are surprised by the share price evolution, but we need to report on already low investor expectations. Given the declining segments and limited visibility for 2027, we continue to recommend a sell.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: XLI: Why The Pullback From All-Time Highs Is A Re-Entry Opportunity
data.description:
The Industrial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLI) tracks the Industrial Select Sector Index, which is a market cap weighted benchmark of S&P 500-classified industrial companies. This spans aerospace and defense, machinery, ground transportation, and commercial services. XLI is the most liquid vehicle for U.S. industrial sector exposure, averaging over $10 million in average daily volume and carrying a 0.08% expense ratio.

I rate XLI a Buy based on two durable, policy-backed spending cycles for the long term. These are defense rearmament and domestic manufacturing reshoring, which are generating multi-year revenue visibility for the fund's largest holdings. This is happening at a time when geopolitical shock and the resulting broader equity market pullback has pushed XLI to a meaningful discount from its March 2026 all-time high.

Industry Investment Thesis

The thesis for XLI rests on three intersecting forces, each with a multi-year duration and, most importantly, the policy backing that limits cyclical reversal risk.

Global Defense Rearmament

Before the Iran conflict was in focus, the conflict between Russia and Ukraine accelerated a structural shift in sovereign defense posture. At the 2025 NATO Summit, allies committed to a target of 5% of GDP annually for core defense requirements by 2035. This is an extremely ambitious goal that reflects serious procurement acceleration across NATO's European members. Germany alone plans to increase its annual defense budget from €86 billion in 2025 to €152 billion by 2029. The US is no different, with Trump having publicly committed to a $1.5 trillion defense budget.

I've written about this at length but it bears repeating that this rearmament cycle is one of the strongest secular trends in the market right now, and I do not see that changing in the near-term.

This is important for investors, as XLI's aerospace and defense allocation makes up almost 27% of the fund.

To give a couple of examples from the biggest names, GE Aerospace's (GE) order backlog exceeded $160 billion in FY2025, and defense primes are guiding between 8–10% EPS CAGR through FY2028.

RTX Corporation (RTX), with its dual exposure to commercial aviation recovery and NATO defense contracts, provides a ballast in this environment.

The conflict in Iran has only added fuel to the fire in this respect, and even with the ceasefire agreed in principle on 4/8, there is no evidence that the war has come to an end. The pressure will be on each nation to secure it's defense in a world full of geopolitical tension and the threat of destabilization rising.

Domestic Manufacturing Reshoring and CapEx

Over the first eight months of the second Trump administration, companies announced over $1.2 trillion in U.S. manufacturing investments, which was layered on top of more than $750 billion in commitments from the prior administration.

The One Big Beautiful Bill Act codified 100% bonus depreciation for capital equipment. This is a permanent incentive that businesses are seeking to capitalize on, but especially companies tracked in this fund. For businesses like Caterpillar Inc. (CAT), Deere & Company (DE), and Eaton Corporation plc (ETN), this spending translates directly into equipment orders.

Grid and energy infrastructure.

GE Vernova Inc. (GEV) makes up 4.65% of XLI. While GEV is a less-discussed holding, is the direct beneficiary of a third secular trend which is, the industrial electrification cycle.

Electricity consumption from the U.S. industrial sector is forecast to increase 3.8% in 2026. This number is set to accelerate and it aligns with the trend we have been seeing as more manufacturing facilities, data centers, and EV infrastructure come online.

One of the more shocking figures in this report from Global X is that U.S. power demand could grow by as much as 50% between 2024 and 2040. For the purposes of this fund, Eaton, which is also in the top 10 holdings, is another business positioned at the intersection of grid modernization and data center power.

Quick Facts on the ETF

The Industrial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLI) launched in 1998 and is managed by State Street Global Advisors. As of this writing, the fund carries roughly $29.5 billion in net assets, and has an average trading volume of about 10 million shares, making it among the most liquid single-sector ETFs in existence. The expense ratio is 0.08%, identical to peers like Vanguard Industrials ETF (VIS) and Fidelity MSCI Industrials Index ETF (FIDU).

XLI tracks the Industrial Select Sector Index, which is built by applying market-cap weighting to S&P 500-classified industrials and rebalancing every quarter. The index used a weighting method that caps individual positions to prevent excessive concentration, though in practice no single name currently exceeds 6.52% of fund assets. In all there are 82 businesses held in the fund across industrial categories.

Holdings, Performance, and Outlook

XLI's top 10 holdings account for approximately 39% of fund assets and are concentrated in three structural themes, each of which are being driven by one or multiple secular trends I mentioned above.

Aerospace & Defense makes up about 27% of the fund and the largest holdings in this sector are the ones you would expect. This includes GE Electric Company (GE) (5.88%), RTX Corporation (5.12%), and The Boeing Company (BA) (3.09%) which anchor this portion of the portfolio.

It is worth mentioning that GE Aerospace's transformation from conglomerate to pure-play aerospace engine manufacturer has been one of the more quietly successful industrial restructurings of the past decade. Its engines power the majority of the world's commercial aircraft, and its aftermarket services business, which are essentially long-term service agreements, generates recurring revenue that makes the business far more resilient than what you would expect from a standard industrial cyclical.

RTX is another giant in this sector with a promising setup. RTX combines Pratt & Whitney engine manufacturing, Collins Aerospace systems, and Raytheon defense electronics as three core segments in it's revenue engine. Its defense backlog is heavily weighted toward NATO-allied procurement, and as we discussed above with defense contracts, extends revenue visibility well beyond the current budget cycle.

Machinery totals about 21% of the fund as a subsector. The biggest names and most recognizable here are Caterpillar (6.52%) and Deere (2.81%), each serving as the machinery anchors of the fund. From an investor's perspective, Caterpillar acts as a a kind of proxy for global infrastructure and resource development spending. Similarly, Deere's precision agriculture and construction equipment segments tie its revenue to farm income and large-scale construction activity, both of which benefit from the reshoring-driven wave of new construction that is still in it's early stages.

Electrification and automation makes up 11% of the fund. GE Vernova (4.65%) and Eaton (2.75%) represent XLI's most direct exposure to the energy transition and grid modernization CapEx cycle. This subsector will mark an important part of the portfolio given the rising demand outlined in the industry investment thesis above, with GE Vernova and Eaton ideally positioned to benefit.

Performance Context

XLI delivered a 1-year return of approximately 30% through early 2026, and a 3-year annualized return of over 20%, outpacing the 18% return for the S&P 500 over the same period.

The 10-year annualized return stands at roughly 14%, comparable to the broader market over the same period.

XLI was trading at all-time highs in March before pulling back over 12% in March, but has rebounded in April, up 6.4% in just the last 10 days.

Per PortfoliosLab, XLI carries a trailing 12-month Sharpe of approximately 1.34 and a Sortino ratio of approximately 1.86, both comparing favorably to the S&P 500 benchmark which carries a trailing 12 Sharpe ratio of 0.99, Sortino of 1.41 over the same period.

The fund's beta of 1.10 versus the S&P 500 somewhat reflects its cyclical nature, although the top holdings have shown to be very resilient and positioned to withstand cyclical downtrends. Even with that said, it is crucial that investors in this fund expect XLI to amplify both upside and downside market moves as shown in the last several years.

Peer Methodology Comparison: XLI vs. VIS vs. PAVE

Three funds offer meaningfully different exposures within U.S. industrials, and the choice matters.

The Vanguard Industrials ETF (VIS) tracks the MSCI US Investable Market Industrials 25/50 Index and the fund holds over 330 businesses. The key difference here is that VIS includes significant mid-cap exposure that is purposely excluded from XLI. In an environment with several geopolitical question marks, I prefer to place a premium on resilience and strength that comes with names in XLI, at least for now.

When you draw up the comparison, you can see that essentially both funds have delivered nearly identical 10-year annualized returns with XLI at 14.01%, VIS at 13.96%, coming with very similar risk levels.

Only a slight negligible difference in cost between the two, as VIS carries a .09% expense ratio, only barely higher than the .08% for XLI. Investors seeking broader industrial exposure, including mid-caps with greater domestic revenue concentration, may prefer VIS. The two funds carry about a 0.99 correlation over rolling periods, so the practical return difference is marginal.

The Global X U.S. Infrastructure Development ETF (PAVE) is different in that it tracks the INDXX U.S. Infrastructure Development Index with 100 holdings, and is notably more skewed toward smaller infrastructure companies that could be considered more pure-play infrastructure, many of which are absent from XLI.

PAVE carries a 0.47% expense ratio and a higher rolling volatility of 5.5% versus XLI's 4.75%. With that said, the fund has clearly outperformed both XLI and VIS, especially in the last several years.

PAVE returned approximately 49% in the trailing 12 months vs. XLI's 38%. The core reason for this recent outperformance is that PAVE's construction and engineering tilt gives it more direct infrastructure buildout exposure, but with greater concentration risk and significantly higher trading costs.

Over the long term, I'm primarily interested in XLI's value proposition as the winner in volatile markets against its peers. This is simply due to is liquidity, scale, and cost efficiency at the large-cap end of the industrial sector. For investors who want a single-ticket, deeply liquid industrials position anchored in S&P 500 constituents, I believe it remains the default choice.

Risks

There are several risks to consider that need to be on any investor's radar. The most glaring and legitimate near-term headwind is geopolitical and trade policy uncertainty. The National Association of Manufacturers reported that 78% of its member respondents cited trade uncertainty as a concern in its Q3 2025 survey. Tariff-related input cost pressure and retaliatory export restrictions could weigh on margins at companies with global supply chains. That tension is real, and investors should weigh it. Keep in mind that XLI's holdings almost universally have global supply chains and international revenue streams. An escalation in reciprocal tariffs or export restrictions (as well as the looming risk of conflict restarting and escalating in the Middle East) could compress margins at Caterpillar, Deere, and RTX, all of which have meaningful non-U.S. revenue. This is a real risk right now.

Obviously this risk comes with cyclicality and the possibility of economic slowdown. XLI is not priced for a recessionary scenario. If U.S. GDP growth slows materially from current expectations, industrial earnings estimates will move down before the fund's price does. The PMI for manufacturing remained below 50 for much of 2025, a signal worth monitoring as leading indicators.

Another clear risk is the concentration that inherently comes with XLI. The top five holdings represent approximately 25% of the fund and earnings disappointments in any of the top holdings listed above will almost certainly move the overall fund on a quarterly earnings beat or miss. As an investor in XLI, I watch GE Aerospace, GE Vernova, RTX, and Caterpillar closely as bellweathers that typically tell a wider story in terms of what markets are pricing in for industrials, and how investors are calculating the strength of the trends driving industrials higher.

Lastly, there is always the risk of defense budget uncertainty. While structural defense spending growth is well-documented, investors have to remember that political changes can happen quickly. The reality is that fiscal constraints and political divisions can always serve to limit further increases in U.S. defense appropriations. There will be serious political battles around these topics, especially as the national debt and deficit spending become increasingly scrutinized and brought under the microscope. Congressional hawks and executive branch priorities have not always aligned, which introduces risk around Pentagon's multi-year modernization plans.

Final Thoughts

XLI earns a Buy rating at current levels.

The pullback from the March 2026 all-time high was driven largely by macro factors and supply chain/trade-policy anxiety rather than any deterioration in the fund's fundamental earnings drivers. This creates an opportunity for investors who missed the initial run.

Defense rearmament, domestic manufacturing reshoring, and the industrial CapEx cycle tied to AI infrastructure are themes that will last several years. These allocation decisions are already reflected in contracted backlogs.

I see a 12-month price target of $185.

This is based on forward earnings assumptions consistent with anywhere between 8–10% EPS growth across the top holdings, and also holding a sector P/E multiple in between the 27–29x range.

From here this implies about a 14% upside from current levels, not including the 1.39% dividend yield.

This is a great allocation for equity investors seeking direct, low-cost exposure to structural U.S. industrial spending themes, and I believe that this is only the early innings of several long-term trends that will push XLI higher.

data.title: U.S.-Iran Ceasefire: What To Expect From Negotiations This Weekend
data.description:
It's all about the Strait of Hormuz

The US-Iran war is currently in a two-week ceasefire. The high-level representatives from each country are expected to start negotiations this weekend in Pakistan.

Let's be clear, these negotiations are strictly about the Strait of Hurmuz, thus very narrow in scope, and not expected to solve the key underlying issues - the Iranian uranium enrichment and the ballistic missiles program.

Leading to the ceasefire, President Trump "angrily" requested the full opening of the Strait of Hormuz via several social media posts and even made some "apocalyptic" threats against the entire Iranian civilization.

President Trump's concerns are understandable - if the Strait of Hormuz remains closed, the oil price shock could cause a global depression, even global famine due to a shortage of fertilizers. Thus, it is absolutely necessary that the Strait of Hormuz reopen.

So, the US and Iran are set to negotiate the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. Before discussing the likely outcome of these negotiations and their effect on the financial markets, it's important to understand the broad picture and underlying reasons for the war.

What each side wants

This war has three sides: the US and Israel versus Iran.

Israel

Isreal primarily wants to eliminate the nuclear threat from Iran - this is an existential threat for Israel. Nobody really knows what Iran's current nuclear capabilities are, outside the intelligence community. So, we have to assume "something is going on" with Iran given the urgency to attack. Israel also understands that regime change in Iran is the only longer-term solution, so this is likely Israel's primary objective.

Iran

Iran's primary objective now is likely regime survival, exactly countering Israel's primary objective. For longer-term regime survival, Iran needs a nuclear weapon, based on the North Korean case, which is exactly what Israel wants to prevent from happening. For near-term survival, Iran's only leverage is the Strait of Hormuz - by closing Hormuz, Iran keeps the global economy as hostage.

The US

The US initially supported Israel's objectives of regime change and nuclear threat. However, the US now has the primary objective of opening the Strait of Hormuz to prevent the US and global depression.

The negotiations in Pakistan

Thus, the US wants Iran to fully reopen the Strait of Hormuz so the oil prices could fall. This would prevent a global depression.

However, for Iran, the Strait of Hormuz is the only leverage for regime survival. Thus, Iran simply cannot allow the Strait of Hormuz to reopen without getting strong guarantees that it will not be attacked again and assurance that the regime will survive.

But these negotiations could produce only a short-term assurance, and any guarantees given can be changed in the future or not enforced. Thus, Iran still needs a nuclear program as longer-term leverage.

However, the nuclear issue will not even be discussed in Pakistan this weekend because the agreement is not possible - it's been tried many times and each time failed. So, we are back in square one.

Likely outcome of negotiations

Iran's current status for the Strait of Hormuz is that it's open for all ships from "non-enemy" countries.

With this status, Iran is possibly charging a transit fee and requires a boat inspection. In practice, only about 10 boats have been crossing Hormuz daily, mostly Iranian, Chinese, and Russian. Before the war, 130-150 boats would cross Hormuz. So, Hormuz is practically closed.

Iran is likely to change the status to: It's open to all countries, but still conditional to an approval after inspection and with the transit fee.

With this new status, Iran gets confirmed control of Hormuz, thus keeping the key short-term leverage. In addition, with inspections, Iran can significantly slow the transit of ships to 30-40 a day, still well below the pre-war levels. Thus, energy prices would still remain high, and inflation would still be a problem, which means Iran would still hold a grip on a global economy.

The US will likely accept this "partial reopening" of the Strait of Hormuz as an immediate solution and extend the ceasefire for another two months, during which the focus will be to negotiate a gradual increase in the number of ships passing Hormuz to over 100.

This partial reopening will be conditional on the ceasefire between Israel and Hezbollah, and Israel is likely to use this "pause" to gain more intelligence on Iran and prepare for an eventual escalation to address the nuclear issue.

Implications for the financial markets

Oil

Crude oil price is still likely to be high, in a range between $80 and $100/barrel for WTI (CL1:COM). The partial reopening will slow the oil exports from the Persian Gulf. The insurance rates for tankers are likely to fall, but still price some war premium, including the transit fee. In addition, the regional energy infrastructure seems to be damaged, and thus, the production will lag over the next few months. Thus, inflation will still be elevated through the summer, possibly reaching 4% for headline CPI.

Bonds

The Federal Funds rates are starting to price a cut in 2026, but this is very unlikely, as even core inflation will be over 3% in 2026 - and inflationary pressures will still be persistent, especially when considering the tariffs. The 10-year yields (US10Y) also currently price a well-anchored inflation. Thus, with persisting inflation pressures due to the partial reopening, the 10Y yield could rise towards the 5% level, especially as the flight-to-quality eases with the ceasefire.

The S&P500

The S&P 500 (SP500) has already priced out the most extreme scenario, and probably much more. The VIX Index has fallen below 20, which suggests that the recent rally was short covering as hedge funds reduced the short positions. Any further rally will have to be PE multiple expansion, which is unlikely because:

  • The economy is slowing, with GDPNow showing only 1.3% growth for the current quarter, which suggests weak earnings growth at best.
  • Inflation is likely to remain elevated, which suggests that the Fed will be in the higher-for-longer mode, and this is not supportive for a multiple expansion.
  • There are other issues capping the rally, such as private credit bust, AI disruption, AI bubble, and upcoming issues with tariffs.

Thus, the S&P 500 could grind higher if the VIX drops to 15, which is unlikely given the combined systems risk. It's more likely that investors will sell the fact, and the S&P 500 will continue to trade in a volatile range with the downside bias.

Bottom line: the US-Iran ceasefire is an opportunity to sell the rally. A possible outcome of negotiations will be only a very short-term patch, without even addressing the key issues.
